title: The dissociator part that checks out during intimacy
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The part that leaves when things get close

You're in bed. Or on the couch. Or maybe just sitting across from someone you care about. Things start to feel warm, connected, real. And then something happens. You're not exactly sure what. But suddenly you're not really there anymore.

Your body might still be present. Maybe you're even going through the motions. But you've checked out. There's a fog between you and the moment. A distance that wasn't there seconds ago. And you didn't choose it. It just happened.

I know this experience because I've lived it. More times than I can count. There's a part of me that handles intimacy by leaving. It's been doing this job for so long that I barely notice it happening anymore. But I'm learning to see it. And I want to help you see yours too.

Here's the thing. This dissociator part isn't trying to ruin your relationships. It's not malicious or broken. It's trying to protect you from something it learned long ago was dangerous. Intimacy. Closeness. Being seen. All of it can feel like a threat when your nervous system learned otherwise.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would call this a dorsal vagal response. It's the oldest, most primitive part of your nervous system. The one that shuts everything down when danger feels overwhelming. Your body freezes. Your mind goes blank. You disconnect. It's not a choice. It's a survival strategy that your system learned before you had words for any of this.

Right? Think about how automatic that feels. One moment you're present. The next you're floating somewhere above the room. Or your mind is suddenly consumed by a random work email from three days ago. Or you feel nothing at all, just a hollow emptiness where connection used to be.

How this part developed its job

This dissociator part didn't appear out of nowhere. It formed because at some point in your life, staying present during intimacy or connection was not safe. Maybe you experienced physical or sexual abuse. Maybe it was emotional neglect. Maybe someone you trusted hurt you in ways you still can't fully name. Or maybe it was more subtle. A parent who couldn't hold your feelings. A caregiver who only showed affection when you performed perfectly. A relationship where your boundaries were constantly ignored.

Whatever the source, your nervous system learned a crucial lesson: closeness equals danger. And when closeness equals danger, the best way to survive is to not be fully there.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has spent decades studying how trauma lives in the body. He talks about dissociation as a fundamental response to overwhelming experience. When you can't fight and you can't flee, you freeze. And freezing isn't just physical stillness. It's mental and emotional absence. Your system pulls you out of your body because staying in your body during that moment would be too much to bear.

The problem is that this response doesn't just activate during the original trauma. It generalizes. Your nervous system starts applying the old rule to new situations. Anyone who gets close. Any moment of real vulnerability. Any experience of being seen and known. The dissociator part kicks in, and you're gone.

Stay with me here. This is where it gets tricky. Because this part is so automatic, so deeply ingrained, that you might not even recognize it as a part. You might think this is just who you are. "I'm not really a sexual person." "I struggle with intimacy." "I just can't seem to stay present during sex." These stories feel true. But they're not the whole truth. They're descriptions of what this part does, not definitions of who you are.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts by Richard Schwartz. It helps you understand that every part of you, even the ones that seem to cause problems, has a positive intention. This dissociator part genuinely believes it's helping you survive.

What this part actually feels like in your body

I want you to take a moment and notice what happens in your body when you think about being truly present during intimacy. Not the idealized version. Not the fantasy. Real intimacy. Where you're fully there, fully seen, fully vulnerable.

Do you feel a tightening somewhere? A heaviness? A desire to change the subject? Maybe your chest gets tight. Maybe your throat closes. Maybe you feel suddenly tired, like you need to sleep right now. Or maybe you just go blank. Nothing. No feeling at all.

That nothingness is the dissociator at work. It's the most effective strategy because it makes you feel nothing when being present would feel too much. Your system has learned to skip over the painful feelings by skipping over presence altogether.

I've had moments during sex where I was technically there but completely gone. My body was moving. Sounds were happening. But I was watching from somewhere far away, disconnected and numb. Afterward, I'd feel shame. Confusion. I'd wonder what was wrong with me. Why couldn't I just be present like everyone else seemed to be?

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors by Janina Fisher. She explains how different parts of us hold different pieces of the trauma, and how dissociation creates these splits. Your dissociator part is holding a very specific piece of the story.

How to start working with this part

The first step is not to get rid of this part. That approach never works anyway. You can't shame a part into disappearing. It only makes it work harder. The first step is to get curious. To notice this part without judgment. To ask it what it's trying to do for you.

I started doing this in small moments. Not during sex at first. That was too intense. I started during everyday moments of connection. Talking with a friend. Sitting close to a partner on the couch. Making eye contact with someone I cared about. I'd notice when I started to drift. And instead of judging myself for it, I'd just say, "Oh, there you are. I see you checking out."

That simple acknowledgment changed something. The part felt seen. It didn't have to work as hard because I wasn't fighting it. I was just noticing it.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty by Jonice Webb. Emotional neglect is a common source of this dissociator part. When your feelings were never acknowledged as a child, your system learns that being present with your own emotions isn't safe. So it checks out.

The next step is to build capacity slowly. Your nervous system needs to learn that intimacy is safe now, and that takes time. You can't rush this. You can't force your way through it. You have to go at the pace your system can handle.

Start with micro-moments of presence. Five seconds of eye contact. One deep breath while staying in your body. A single moment of feeling your partner's hand on your skin without checking out. That's it. That's enough. Build from there.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery by Judith Herman. She describes how recovery happens in stages. First comes safety and stabilization. Then remembrance and mourning. Then reconnection. You don't skip to the end. You start where you are, with the part that checks out, and you build safety first.

What healing looks like, and what it doesn't

Healing this part doesn't mean you'll never dissociate again. That's not realistic, and honestly, it's not the goal. The goal is relationship. A different relationship with this part. One where you're not at war with it. One where you can notice it showing up and choose how to respond instead of being controlled by it.

Sometimes you'll still check out during intimacy. That's okay. It doesn't mean you're failing. It means your nervous system is still learning. And learning takes time. Repetition. Patience.

But over time, you might notice something shifting. The dissociator part doesn't have to work as hard. It trusts you a little more. It knows you can handle being present, even when it's uncomfortable. And sometimes, in small moments, you'll find yourself fully there. Present. Connected. Alive in your body. And that feeling, when it comes, is worth everything.

I won't tell you this is easy. It's not. Some days it's brutal. Some days you'll feel like you're taking ten steps back. But I will tell you this: the fact that you're here, reading this, trying to understand this part of yourself, that's already a step toward healing. That's your whole self reaching toward wholeness, even when the dissociator part wants to keep you safe by keeping you small.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did something shift? Did you feel a little more present? Or did the dissociator part step in and take you away again? Either answer is okay. Just notice. That's all. Just notice.

Healing Journey Resources

  • No Bad Parts by Richard Schwartz - A gentle introduction to Internal Family Systems and understanding all parts of yourself.
  • Trauma and Recovery by Judith Herman - The foundational text on trauma recovery and the stages of healing.
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors by Janina Fisher - Understanding how trauma creates different parts and how to work with them.
  • Running on Empty by Jonice Webb - For understanding emotional neglect and its lasting effects on connection and intimacy.
